stories.title: Knights, the UK’s leading regional legal and professional services business, has strengthened its Private Client offering in Sheffield with the appointment of Gillian Coverley as partner.
stories.body:
Gillian joins Knights following more than twenty years at Irwin Mitchell, where she was an equity partner and held several senior leadership roles. Having built her entire career in Sheffield, she brings a strong regional network alongside extensive experience advising highnetworth individuals, professionals and business owners on wills, estate and succession planning, complex probate matters and trust administration.
Her work frequently involves advising clients with complex family circumstances as well as business owners, navigating succession and inheritance tax planning.
Gillian also works closely with charities and their supporters, advising on philanthropic giving, legacy planning and the management of charitable estates.
Gillian chose to join Knights to focus on client delivery, drawn by the firm’s model, which enables senior lawyers to spend more time advising clients and developing relationships, supported by dedicated inhouse teams.
She is a full member of the Society of Trust and Estate Practitioners (STEP) and was the only private client lawyer to be ranked Band 1 in the Chambers High Net Worth guide (2025) for Private Wealth Law in Sheffield and the surrounding area, recognising her as one of the leading advisers in the region.
Commenting on her appointment, Gillian says: “I was attracted to the Knights model as it allows for partners to dedicate their time working closely with clients and building relationships.
“Having worked in Sheffield throughout my career, I’m looking forward to building on established connections and supporting clients through increasingly complex estate and succession planning issues, as well as developing the firm’s offering for charities and their supporters.”
David Lister, Sheffield client services director, added: “Gillian is a highly respected private client adviser with a longstanding reputation in the Sheffield market.
“Her depth of experience, regional connections and strong clientfocused approach mak e her an excellent fit for Knights.”
Gillian’s appointment further strengthens the private client team in Sheffield, underlining its commitment to investing in highcalibre people who add real value for clients.
Knights is the largest legal and professional services business built for the UK regions. With 1,400 professionals working collaboratively nationwide, Knights provides multiple services to business clients alongside premium advisory services to private individuals.

stories.title: Harlow Consulting appoints new research manager
stories.body:
Mary Meredith joins the agency to support its mixed-methods research and evaluation services, working across key sectors including construction, heritage, healthcare, education and the arts.
Prior to Harlow Consulting, Mary has held roles in marketing, organisational research and intelligence, and specialist HR roles.
Harlow Consulting works with leading organisations in the UK. Clients include Historic England, the Food Standards Agency, the NHS, Innovate UK, the Department for Culture, Media and Sport, Skills Development Scotland, and the British Film Institute (BFI). 
Managing projects from start to completion, Mary’s strengths lie in qualitative research and evidence-led storytelling.
Mary said: “Words have always been a big part of my life, whether through reading, writing or storytelling in all its forms. I’m curious, analytical and enjoy untangling problems. Working across different sectors and disciplines has given me a wide-ranging perspective that helps me approach projects in a thoughtful and creative way.”
Harlow Consulting was founded in 2019 by co-directors Jennifer Brennan and Clare Vokes.
The female-led consultancy has made waves in the world of research, growing the agency into one of Yorkshire’s ’50 Most Exciting Companies’ last year. The business maintains a strong focus on team wellbeing and giving back to environmental and community causes.
Jennifer said: “Mary brings diverse skills to our team. She has an impressive background, and her communication skills are particularly valued as our agency continues to evolve. Mary has a passion and flair for understanding the human factors that influence organisational decision-making and change.”
Mary added: “I’m driven by the positive impacts good research has driving practical action and positive change. It can have lasting impacts for public and private sector organisations in fields ranging from health to heritage. It’s also a huge draw working in a female-led business, with a focus on being a flexible, equitable and supportive workplace.”
As well as offering a quarterly wellbeing allowance and regular Wellbeing Days to employees, Harlow Consulting is a Disability Confident and Mindful Employer. The business has signed the Market Research Society Net Zero Pledge and works with Positive Planet to maintain its Carbon Reduction Plan.

stories.title: Leeds Pride has unveiled the final line-up of performers for its landmark 20th anniversary celebration this summer, with more acts than ever before set to take to the stage across a fabulous two-day festival.
stories.body:
Headlining the weekend will be La Voix and Sweet Female Attitude. They’ll be joined by, Tomara Thomas, Livin’ Joy ft. Luzahnn, and Baga Chipz, alongside a whole host of other exciting names.
Presenting the festivities this year are Bradley Riches, Soraya Vivian, drag icons Mamma Bear and St Sordid Secret.
Taking place on Saturday 18 and Sunday 19 July, the milestone event will see the city come alive with its biggest and boldest Pride to date. Organisers are expecting record-breaking crowds for the packed programme of entertainment which will take place from 12pm – 10pm on both days.
The weekend promises to be the most diverse and inclusive in the festival’s history, with a major focus on supporting emerging and local talent across multiple stages.
In a new addition for 2026, Leeds Pride will host The Freedom Party, a ticketed Saturday event, helping to secure the future of the festival for years to come. Revellers can expect a high energy day featuring incredible stage shows and some of the UK’s best tribute acts, paying homage to the hit songs and pop icons of the last 20 years.
Tickets for The Freedom Party are available now from just £7.50, offering incredible value for a full day of live entertainment. Once inside, attendees can enter and exit the site freely throughout the day using their wristband.

This year also introduces a new and improved VIP area on Lower Briggate, alongside an expanded festival footprint, enhanced production and a wider range of experiences for visitors.
Alongside the main stage programme, a full schedule of fringe events will return, including the much-loved BIBIS Fundraising Ball on Friday July 3. Also returning for 2026 are the Drag Brunch Pride Special at Fibre, street parties, community-led performances and not forgetting the iconic Pride parade.
Festivalgoers can also expect a bustling event atmosphere with more food vendors and street food stalls than ever before, alongside local market traders, glitter artists, face painting, art, prints and more.
Jet2, the UK’s largest tour operator, returns as headline sponsor for the third consecutive year, and purchasers of the Leeds Pride wristbands can save £100 on a Jet2holiday as well as gain access to a range of exclusive perks and offers.
While Sunday remains free to attend, organisers are encouraging visitors to show their support by purchasing Saturday tickets in advance.
A spokesperson for Leeds Pride said: ““Celebrating 20 years of Leeds Pride is a huge moment for the city and the community. This year’s line-up is our biggest ever, with more acts, more local talent and more to experience across the entire weekend.
“What we have in Leeds is genuinely rare, and that’s a Pride celebration that takes place right in the heart of the city. While many other Pride events are moving out to parks and greenfield sites, we’re proud to keep Leeds Pride woven into the fabric of the city. It creates an incredible atmosphere and means the celebration is shared across Leeds, supporting hotels, bars, restaurants and local businesses throughout the weekend.
“Pride remains as important as ever, and introducing a ticketed Saturday allows us to protect and grow the event for the future. We’re asking people to support us where they can, even a small contribution helps us continue delivering Pride for everyone.
“We can’t wait to welcome Leeds and visitors from across the UK to what will be an unforgettable weekend.”
Leeds Pride is still welcoming sponsors, parade entries and stall applications, with opportunities available for organisations and community groups to get involved.

Leeds Pride reveals full star-studded line up for 20th anniversary celebration

stories.title: Azets welcomes new audit partner in Yorkshire
stories.body:
Sarah joins the firm’s Leeds office after more than 17 years at PwC where she led complex audits for medium to large private, private equity-backed and internationally owned entities.
She has experience in a broad range of sectors and counts retail and manufacturing as a specialist interest, delivering insight alongside practical, hands-on support.
Sarah will work across Yorkshire delivering high-quality services, underpinned by trusted relationships, to help clients grow and manage increasing regulatory demands.
Claire Needham, who is regional managing director for Yorkshire, said: “A very warm welcome to Sarah. She brings a wealth of experience and expertise built over many years delivering exceptional audit and assurance services for a diverse range of businesses.
“I am confident that she will make  a major contribution to the continued success of our expanding audit team in Yorkshire and the UK.”
Sarah graduated with a degree in Mathematics with Finance from the University of Leeds in 2008. She  held positions ranging from associate to director during her career, before taking her partner role at Azets.
Sarah said: “I am thrilled to join the superb audit and assurance team at Azets. My collaborative and relationship-driven approach matches the firm’s own values and commitment to delivering high-quality services and the best outcomes through a genuine understanding of each client’s business, challenges and ambitions. 
“I‘m looking forward to helping clients navigate expansion, change and compliance with confidence while playing my part in the growth of Azets.”
Outside of work, Sarah enjoys spending time in the countryside with her young family, good food with friends and family and running.
Azets, a UK top 10 accountancy and business advisory firm, has offices in King Street in Leeds as well as offices in York and Bradford where it employs 335 people in total.

stories.title: Leeds law firm Ward Hadaway has appointed Alex Cooper as a corporate partner as it continues to build its corporate offering in Yorkshire.
stories.body:
The appointment forms part of ongoing investment in the firm’s corporate team which was named the most active legal adviser in Yorkshire and the Humber last year.
Alex joins with more than 20 years’ experience in corporate transactional work, advising on UK and international mergers and acquisitions, disposals, investments and restructurings. This follows the recent addition of banking and finance specialist Aarti Parmar as a director in the Leeds corporate team.
Alex said: “I’m pleased to have joined the team at Ward Hadaway. Not only does it consistently top the lists of most active dealmakers in Yorkshire and the Humber, but the firm also punches well above its weight at a national level, and it’s exciting to be part of such a successful and ambitious business.
 “We have genuine strength and depth in our team which will allow us to continue to deliver high quality outcomes for a diverse range of clients across the region.”
 Nik Tunley, head of corporate said: “We have built a market-leading mid-market M&A and finance practice, recognised nationally for its expertise, credibility and consistent delivery of high-quality outcomes. Our focus remains on strengthening that reputation further, supported by the breadth and depth of capability we offer across our offices.
“The arrival of Alex and Aarti in Leeds significantly enhances the scale and experience of our offering, enabling us to support clients through increasingly complex and high-value transactions. Their addition reinforces our commitment to a partner-led service, combining clear, pragmatic advice with the consistently high standards that define our M&A and finance practice.”
The firm has seen sustained levels of deal activity and was recently ranked among the UK’s most active legal advisers in 2025 in Experian MarketIQ’s latest UK and Republic of Ireland M&A Review. 
Steven Petrie, managing partner said: “We have continued to invest in our corporate team over the past 12 months through senior appointments and have strengthened our already impressive corporate offering nationally.
 “Alex brings significant experience in mid-market transactions and adds further depth to the team as we continue to grow, with more developments in the pipeline.”

Ward Hadaway strengthens Leeds corporate team with partner appointment

stories.title: “All right twinkle toes, what’s your exit strategy?” So said Tom Arnold’s character Albert Gibson to the all-conquering spy Harry Tasker (played by Arnold Schwarzenegger) in the 1994 movie True Lies. It’s a question business owners should be asking themselves too, not just those who have already made the decision to sell up, but all business owners.  For leaving it late to draw up a viable exit plan can narrow down the options available which in turn can lead to a less favourable outcome being achieved.
stories.body:
For many, selling all or part of a business is one of the biggest decisions they will ever make. Sound planning plays a fundamental role in most business successes—it’s what has got you to where you are today along with years, or even decades, of effort and dedication— but for many, the planning only goes so far.  For many, it does not extend to exiting. How many? According to a 2019 study by UBS, around half of business owners surveyed did not have a formal exit strategy in place.
All too often owners being underprepared for a sale can have material consequences. This can lead to selling their businesses either in a manner that does not fully compensate them for their years of effort, or the sale takes place at a later point in time than they expect.
A simple solution: plan early
Planning early, however, can be easier said than done. Owners tend to be so focused on the running and growing of their businesses that they can neglect longer-term personal plans. And there is no off-the-shelf plan to draw on when the time comes either. That’s because no two entrepreneurs are alike. Key considerations and plans therefore depend largely on what matters most to each individual. This can be a difficult process, and a good starting point is simply identifying what’s personally important to the owner by answering the following questions:

What would you like to accomplish?
Who matters most to you?
What legacy would you like to leave?

The answers require careful consideration and may change in time but giving them some thought can help to frame decisions in the present, as well as for the future. Popular routes for those looking to wind down after selling a business include retirement, travel and spending more time with family.  Whereas those looking to carry on working may consider starting another business or becoming an employee for another company.

How much is enough?
Answering the above questions (or at the very least considering them), allows business owners to have a clearer idea of what they want to get out of a sale or other form of exit when the time comes. This helps inform the answer to another key question: how much is enough? Most business owners have a minimum price at which they would sell but three other factors are also key:
Liquidity: What are the resources required to fund your desired lifestyle? Do you have other sources of income? Will your spending increase or decrease going forward? Have you got a cash buffer for unforeseen developments?
Longevity: The length of expected life after selling the business –different for a 40-year-old than a 70-year-old.
Legacy – What you would like to leave behind for your loved ones? This can mean quite different things for different people.
Answers can vary depending on the aims identified in the pre-sale phase. Retirement and travel require higher sale proceeds than if going into employment for another company, for example. True, the calculations can be quite technical—essentially determining the net present value of future family spending—but the results can provide a degree of clarity which would otherwise not have been possible. And while the results may show a shortfall between the attainable sale value and the desired amount, they could also do the opposite, allowing owners to realise they are able to sell sooner than they expected.
So, whether you are thinking of selling your business or you plan to keep going for a good while longer, it’s time to ask yourself: “what’s my exit strategy?”

By Robin  Howcroft Financial Planner at Quilter Cheviot
E: Robin.Howcroft@quiltercheviot.com
T:  0113 513 3973

Beyond the deal: why the best exits start years before sale By Robin Howcroft

stories.title: Wakefield’s historic civic buildings to become thriving new heart for creativity and culture
stories.body:
The project is being delivered by Yorkshire-based property investment and heritage specialists Rushbond, in partnership with Wakefield Council, as part of a wider programme to restore historic buildings and bring new homes, creative workspaces and cultural activity back into this part of the city centre.
The Grade II listed building, to be known as 71 Northgate, will be converted into a series of flexible studio spaces, for makers, artists and small businesses.
The building sits just 100 metres from Wakefield’s historic former Crown Court, to be known ahead as the Court House, where restoration works are already underway. Together, the two buildings form an important part of the Civic Quarter – an area where historic civic buildings are being restored and brought back into everyday use, with new homes, studios and creative spaces returning life to this part of the city centre.
Harry Braid development surveyor at Rushbond said: “The Court House is set to become Wakefield’s most vibrant new destination. It will be a place where everyone belongs - from designers, local radio hosts, independent businesses - all collaborating in the studios; to families enjoying live theatre, music and markets in the Village Hall, all while sharing incredible food and drink. The opportunity to breathe new life into this historic space, together with 71 Northgate is incredibly exciting.” 
Within the wider Civic Quarter vision, 71 Northgate will act as the ‘little sister’ to the Court House, providing smaller-scale studio and enterprise space that complements the larger cultural and events uses planned nearby.
Meanwhile, restoration of Wakefield’s Grade II listed former Crown Court continues, with specialist teams working carefully to preserve the building’s historic features as part of its transformation.
Behind the scenes, specialist teams have been carefully stripping back later additions to reveal and, where possible, salvage original architectural details that will be retained as the building is restored. This reflects the careful, considerate approach required when working with historic buildings of this significance and, in this case, a once-in-a-hundred-years restoration.
When complete, the Court House will reopen to the public for the first time in more than three decades, creating a place for people to gather, work, spend time and enjoy the building as part of everyday city life. The revitalised building also will be a completely new interpretation of the local ‘village hall’ for the new residents who will move into the area.
Jonathan Maud, founder and chairman of Rushbond, said: “Wakefield has some remarkable historic buildings and they deserve to be restored with care, patience and high standards of design and craftsmanship. Projects like the Court House and 71 Northgate are not everyday developments, they are once-in-a-generation opportunities to find sustainable new uses from buildings that have played an important role in the life of the city and aim to do so in the future.
“Our shared ambition for the Civic Quarter is to create something genuinely best in class in terms of build quality, design and the way these historic spaces are used by everyone in the community. Wakefield deserves that level of attention and ambition, and we hope these projects help lift expectations for what is possible in this part of the city centre.”
Elsewhere in the Civic Quarter, the transformation of the surrounding neighbourhood is already visible.
The first phase of 63 homes at the Wood Street Collection was completed last year, bringing new homes into the city centre through the restoration of historic buildings alongside contemporary new housing. The development includes the restored police station apartments, townhouses at Gills Yard and the transformation of Wood House – once the police superintendent’s residence – into a four-storey townhouse.
Six months on, the former Wood Street Police Station is now almost 80% let with just seven apartments remaining to rent. Homes to buy on Gills Mews have also recently been released, with three story townhouses on Rishworth Street available now.

stories.title: Breaking the mould of traditional law firms
stories.body:
Juliet has had a very varied career, from working in pharmaceuticals to sales, and training. she even ran her own cake company ‘Shakey Cakey’ whilst her children were very young. “I love baking and it gave me the experience of setting up my own company and the highs and lows that brings,” she explained.
“What I didn’t fully appreciate was the foundations I was building for launching a bigger company.  We then reached a point where my husband Simon felt that he was at a crossroads in his own career. He was working in a large law firm and felt suffocated and wasn’t happy. He didn’t fit the traditional mould of law firms and wanted something more.”
Both were ready for a change and decided to launch their own law firm with its own values and culture. As Simon had previously changed roles, his clients made the decision to follow him. “He didn’t want to recreate what he had come from,” she continued “and whilst employment law isn’t ‘fun’ it shouldn’t stop you from enjoying your job.
“It has been a challenge effectively going up against such big names, but we stand out because we are different, colourful and fun.”
Juliet is now the CEO of Robinson Ralph and has proved you don’t have to be a lawyer to run a law firm. “Initially I used to apologise for not being a lawyer, but it is exactly that that makes me perfect for the role. Lawyers are great at law – that is what they do, but running a company requires a completely different skill set,” she added.
“I was conscious of this from the start and regularly felt like I had imposter syndrome. I could see the external perception of ‘what is she doing’, but I embody everything it takes to run a company. My focus for the firm is the marketing, the growth and providing different revenue streams. We don’t just give legal advice; we also offer training and practical tools to help businesses handle workplace issues with confidence.
“I am an early riser, and I start my day walking through the woods to school and gives me time without interruptions like phone calls and emails. This gives me focus for the day ahead, whether I am in the office or working from home. I still love baking and contribute cakes to a local community café nearby. It’s a small way for me to stay involved locally and do something I genuinely enjoy.
“The aim for our business is to continue growing and diversifying. We now have 8 members of staff and hope to reach ten by the end of this year. Simon and the lawyers focus on the law and clients, my role is pretty much everything else. We also want to build the additional services we offer such as the training and mediation.  With tribunal waiting lists reaching as long as 3 years before cases are heard, the more mediation we can do, will help to resolve cases before they reach tribunal and the court process.
“As the company grows, I would also like to step back and help other women in particular set up their own law firms.  I would do the hand holding and first few steps support, and based on my own experience, help them to avoid some of the mistakes I made along the way,” she concluded.

WHERE MEDITERRANEAN FLAVOUR MEETS YORKSHIRE CHARM

stories.title: Ramsdens Solicitors has announced a new partner to its family team advising clients from its Leeds office as the firm continues to invest in its regional expansion.
stories.body:
Siobhan Jeffels joins from law firm Clarion, where she worked for five years as legal director.
With more than two decades of family law experience, she specialises in advising clients across complex and high-net-worth finance cases, often involving business owners and farming families undergoing separation.
Siobhan is a trained in Collaborative Law and an active member of Resolution, the national body of family lawyers which promotes a non-confrontational approach to family law issues, and sits on the organisation’s North, East and West Yorkshire committee as treasurer.
Commenting on her appointment, Siobhan said: “It’s a great opportunity to join Ramsdens, which has an impressive regional reputation and has achieved significant recognition over the years for providing outstanding client outcomes across the family team and wider practices.”
“I’m looking forward to collaborating with colleagues who combine leading expertise with a close, personal touch, which is essential for the complex, sensitive cases the team handles.”
Welcoming Siobhan,  Sarah Ward head of family law said, “Having Siobhan onboard is a great addition not only to the family team, but also to the wider firm as we look to maintain regional growth momentum in Leeds and York. Her collaborative approach and impressive track record advising business owners and agricultural families further strengthen our offering in handling complex separation cases.”
“It will also be a great asset as we enhance our client offering as a full-service law firm, ensuring that clients benefit from a single, trusted legal firm for both private and commercial matters.”

Law firm strengthens family team with partner hire

stories.title: What business owners need  to know about upcoming pension reforms
stories.body:
When you own a business, it should be considered as part of your wider estate planning. A properly prepared Will can ensure your business interests are passed on in accordance with your wishes – whether that involves transferring the business to a co-owner, leaving shares to specific individuals, or safeguarding assets through the use of trusts.
What is a Will and why is it important?
A Will is a legal document that sets out who is responsible for administering your estate after your death and how your assets should be distributed. This can include money, property, possessions, business interests, and even your funeral arrangements.
Having a valid Will is a key element of estate planning. It allows you to retain control over how your estate is handled rather than relying on the rules of law. Without one, your assets may be distributed in a way you would not have chosen.
If you die without a valid Will in the UK, you are considered intestate. In this situation, your estate is divided according to intestacy rules, which follow a strict hierarchy of inheritance, typically prioritising a spouse or civil partner, then children, and then other family members.
This can lead to complications, particularly where family dynamics are complex or where business assets require careful succession planning. A valid Will ensures your wishes are clearly documented and can help prevent disputes.
Estate planning and reducing Inheritance Tax
In addition to making a Will, seeking professional advice can help structure your estate more efficiently, particularly where business assets are involved.
One common method of reducing Inheritance Tax (IHT) is through lifetime gifting. You can give away up to £3,000 each tax year without it being subject to IHT under the annual exemption. Gifts above this threshold may still fall within your estate for tax purposes if you pass away within seven years.
You can also make smaller exempt gifts of up to £250 per person each tax year, as well as wedding or civil partnership gifts of up to £5,000 to a child, £2,500 to a grandchild, or £1,000 to another individual.
Charitable donations left in your Will are exempt from IHT. In addition, if you leave more than 10% of your estate to charity, the rate of IHT applied to the remainder of your taxable estate can reduce from 40% to 36%.
Business owners should also be aware of Business Property Relief (BPR), which can significantly reduce or eliminate IHT on qualifying business assets. Some assets qualify for 100% relief, while others qualify for 50%, depending on their nature.
From 6 April 2026, the availability of 100% BPR will be restricted to a combined limit of £2.5 million across qualifying business assets. Any value above this threshold will only qualify for 50% relief, which may increase potential IHT liabilities for larger estates.
Pension changes from 2027
From April 2027, unused pension funds and certain death benefits will be brought into estates for Inheritance Tax calculations.
At present, pensions generally sit outside the estate for IHT purposes. However, from 2027, the value of some pensions may cause individuals to exceed the IHT threshold. The nil-rate band will remain at £325,000, with a potential additional £175,000 residence nil-rate band where a qualifying property is passed to direct descendants.
Exemptions for pension death benefits paid to a surviving spouse or civil partner, as well as death-in-service benefits, are expected to continue to fall outside the estate.
For business owners who have relied on pensions as part of their succession and estate planning, these changes could have a significant impact.
Why act now?
With BPR changes in 2026 and pension changes in 2027, now is the right time to review your estate planning. This means ensuring your Will is up to date, assessing the value of business and pension assets, and considering whether your current plans still meet your long-term objectives.

Chadwick Lawrence can help you navigate these changes, protect your family, preserve business continuity, and reduce future tax exposure. With eight offices across West Yorkshire, the team is accessible to businesses that are looking for support in the region and beyond.

Chadwick Lawrence can help you navigate these changes, protect your family, preserve business continuity, and reduce future tax exposure. With eight offices across West Yorkshire, the team is accessible to businesses that are looking for support in the region and beyond.

stories.title: Growing up, Jamila Juma-Ware wore many hats when it came to deciding what she wanted to do when she left education. “In the morning I would want to be a ballerina, then perhaps a schoolteacher in the afternoon and then a police officer by the evening, but always envisioned being busy,” she told us.
stories.body:
After leaving school though, Jamila headed off to The University of Leeds to study French and music, but following a change of heart, switched and graduated from Leeds Met in politics.
Her first job was in public relations and for a long time worked in a variety of in-house teams as head of marketing and in agencies in PR and events, most recently running her own freelance agency, Creative Marmalade, focussing on promoting large scale events to global audiences, including Dubai, South Africa, Washington DC and London.
“Setting up my own business wasn’t difficult, I think anyone can set up a business, but getting the strategy right and having the courage to take the leap from paid employment, to trusting other sources to pay, and on time is the hardest part, and the biggest risk,” she explained. “I’m a pretty risk averse person, but I was lucky enough to have the support of clients in the early days, requesting my marketing service freelance, which led me on my first steps of entrepreneurship. I also had the heavy support of my fiancé, who encouraged me to see my own strengths and believe in them, which helps when you’re making a decision that is outside of your normal comfort zone.
‘Setting up my hospitality and spirit business came a little easier, as I was already working for myself, and in some sense had the perfect side hustle to help bootstrap the business, especially in the early days,” she added. “I’d been supporting one of my PR clients with event management support, including running a themed pop-up bar (with dry ice and a little magic!). My fiancé Matthew had been a travelling tailor for almost 10 years, and we started out by finding the perfect space to transform into a tailor’s studio by day and a 1920s cocktail bar by night. Tying everything, we do back to a solid strategy, we looked at elements that would make use truly the most authentic and unique speakeasy – and so began our journey in distilling spirits.”
Jamila’s first business set up 11 years ago, the first bar Tailors Speakeasy has been running since 2018 with the original spirit brand Tailors Gin and the full luxury spirit range launched for retail in 2020. A second venue, a literary themed bar and kitchen called Writers’ Bloc launched in Haworth. “I’ve always dreamed of being my own boss and having the flexibility to spend time with family, as much as to build and mould my own future.”
So, what’s a typical day like for Jamila? “Managing a team of 14 and three interconnected businesses means no two days are the same. Day’s range from overseeing operation of both venues, stock, website and social media management to processing retailer and consumer orders for our spirits.
“We are currently looking at our third venue, there are a couple of wild cards up our sleeve which are pretty exciting, but essentially, we want to build and  expand the business footprint and help develop our team alongside this, building opportunity for  not just us, but our fantastic  team that support the operation.”

Mixing suits with spirits

stories.title: What do you know about the capital of the county?
stories.body:
York was founded in 71 CE by the Roman Ninth Legion and named Eboracum. Its strategic position at the confluence of the rivers Ouse and Foss, made it a vital military and administrative centre. Emperors such as Septimius Severus ruled from York and it was here that Constantine the Great was proclaimed emperor in 306 CE. Today, remains of Roman walls and roads still underpin parts of modern York as we know it today.
After the Romans withdrew in the early 5th century, York declined before re-emerging as an important Anglo-Saxon settlement. In 866 Viking forces captured the city and renamed it Jorvik. Under Norse rule, York became a thriving trade hub connecting England with Scandinavia and beyond.

Following the Norman conquest of 1066, York became the administrative centre of Northern England. The construction of York Minster, one of the largest gothic cathedrals in Europe, symbolised its religious importance. The city walls, much of which still stand today, were expanded during this period.
By the 16th and 17th centuries, York remained influential but began to lose economic ground to emerging industrial centres. During the English civil war, the city was a royalist stronghold and endured a significant siege in 1644.
Although York was not a major industrial powerhouse like neighbouring Manchester and Leeds, it adapted through industries such as railways and confectionery. Companies like Rowntree’s and Terry’s of York became nationally recognised brands. The arrival of the railway in the 19th century transformed York into a key transport hub, reinforcing its regional importance. Did you know that the building that now houses The Grand Hotel, just a short walk from the main station, was originally the headquarters of the railway in York and Queen Victoria famously stopped in York for tea whilst passing through on a train, at The Milner Hotel, situated within the train station complex.
York is famous as the UK’s original ‘chocolate city’ due to its rich 300-year history of cocoa manufacturing. This legacy was built by pioneering quaker families who revolutionised the industry, transforming chocolate from a luxury drink for the elite, into an affordable treat for the masses.  Some of the world’s most famous chocolate bars were invented in York. Rowntree’s introduced staples like the KitKat, Aero, Smarties, Quality Street and the Yorkie Bar. Terry’s is globally renowned for the Terry’s Chocolate Orange.
Today, York keeps its sweet heritage alive. The Nestle factory on Haxby Road still produces millions of KitKats daily and visitors can experience the city’s legacy firsthand by visiting the interactive York’s chocolate story museum or taking hands-on workshops at York Cocoa Works.
Modern York is celebrated for its heritage and tourism. Visitors are drawn to its preserved medieval streets like The Shambles, the city walls, its famous racecourse that hosts the annual Ebor festival and was the chosen racecourse by the late Queen replacing Royal Ascot, when Royal Ascot was being refurbished and the city’s museums. Today, the city balances historical preservation with a vibrant cultural life, making it one of England’s most distinctive destinations.

stories.title: June saw the launch of a new aparthotel in York, a thoughtful and sustainable 21st century response to the medieval history of Micklegate, combining modern comfort with a strong sense of place and heritage.
stories.body:
The 46-suite hotel in Micklegate, which has been designed by leading York architects brown + company, features self-contained apartment units that are aimed at people having longer stays in York.  
Blossom Street-based architects brown + company have worked on major York developments such as Coney Street Riverside and the remodeling of the campus at St Peter’s School.
Roger Walters, founder and chairman of Supercity, commented: “Our design team have worked very closely with the team at brown + company. The original exterior was agreed with the City of York Council after careful deliberation to make the building blend in with the character of York.
“It has been a pleasure to work with brown + company.  They have supported us throughout the construction and planning stages. I highly recommend the practice and will use them on our next project.”
This successful development in York is testimony to brown + company’s established practice of working seamlessly with best-in-class partners to deliver unique solutions for complex projects. brown + company worked collaboratively with Montagu Evans, London-based townscape and heritage consultants.
The aparthotel’s surroundings were a key influence on its medieval-inspired design. Micklegate has long presented a streetscape rich in architectural diversity, with the former Roman road playing a significant role throughout each of York’s historical eras.
The name Micklegate, meaning “Great Street,” is derived from the Old Norse Myglagata, while Micklegate Bar later became an important element of the city’s medieval defenses. During the medieval period, a substantial Benedictine Priory dominated the south-east side of the street, while, over time, the north-west side evolved to accommodate the elegant Georgian townhouses that remain today.
brown + company’s design strategy was to create a contemporary 21st-century building that responds sensitively to the context of this historically significant streetscape. The objective was to deliver a solution that enhances the area without adversely affecting the conservation area or its adjacent listed buildings. Working within stringent planning and heritage requirements made this a particularly rewarding project for the architectural team, whose aim was to manage change while preserving the site’s cultural significance.
The design approach drew inspiration from the medieval jettied timber-framed buildings that once occupied the south-east side of Micklegate. A jettied timber-framed building is a medieval construction form in which upper floors project beyond the floor below, creating additional internal space without encroaching on the streets beneath. The scheme also carefully considered rooftop views from York’s historic city walls, as well as key viewpoints along Micklegate itself.
To successfully deliver this complex project, brown + company worked closely with its respected national partner, Leach Rhodes Walker. Their expertise in resolving challenging technical details contributed to a streamlined project experience and successful delivery for the client.
The completed sustainable building preserves Micklegate’s architectural and historic character while delivering a scheme that creates meaningful urban value through placemaking. The development contributes to the continued regeneration of Micklegate, helping to position the street once again at the forefront of York’s tourism and leisure economy.
The aparthotel was initially due to open in 2023 but was delayed by almost a year after part of York’s original medieval wall was found at the site. The site had to be excavated with archaeologists finding items which dated back to the Roman period.
Meanwhile Roger Walters said 12.5 full-time and four part-time jobs will be created by the new hotel. He added that he felt there were few options for businesspeople to make their temporary base while staying in York for longer periods, despite the city’s high number of short-term let properties.
He also hopes the new aparthotel’s city centre location – walking distance from the railway station – will encourage people to stay in York while working nearby, which will help boost the local economy.
“It’s going to bring in a bit more permanency to the visitors, so people will hang around for a few days rather than come in and out of York,” he said.
The new aparthotel features 46 beautifully designed apartment suites that seamlessly combine luxury, practicality, and comfort. Each suite is thoughtfully curated, showcasing a distinctive art collection and original artifacts uncovered during the building’s development, adding a touch of historical charm to the modern amenities.
Facilities include: Lifts; 24 Hour Front Desk; Free Wifi; and easily accessible rooms.

Medieval-inspired York aparthotel opens in York

stories.title: Yorkshire women in the rural landscape star at Great Yorkshire Show
stories.body:
Hardy and Free has been created by award-winning photographer Carolyn Mendelsohn and takes its name from a line in Emily Bronte’s Wuthering Heights: “I wish I were a girl again, half-savage and hardy, and free...” It was originally commissioned by the Brontë Parsonage Museum in Haworth as part of its contemporary arts programme and then a new series of portraits was created for Bradford 2025, UK City of Culture.
Hardy and Free will be on show in the building next to the Ariat GYS Stage from Wednesday to Friday and explores the relationship between 12 contemporary women and the natural world, all sharing a profound emotional link with the landscapes that shape them.
Over several seasons, Carolyn photographed her subjects, artists, swimmers, athletes, adventurers and activists, in their chosen natural locations. She also recorded their thoughts on why those places were important to them.
From remote moorlands to urban parks, waterfalls to working farms, these environments serve as a backdrop to narratives of resilience, creativity and identity.
Caroyln said: “I am delighted that my series Hardy and Free is being exhibited at the Great Yorkshire Show. It feels especially apt that work made in collaboration with women from Yorkshire, in landscapes that hold deep personal meaning for them, is being presented at an event so closely connected to agricultural life and experienced by thousands of visitors.
“The journey of the series reflects this  spirit first shown on an intimate scale at the Brontë Parsonage Museum, then expanded outdoors in the urban centre of Bradford as part of its City of Culture programme and now brought indoors again at large scale within the setting of the Show.
“My hope is that at the Great Yorkshire Show more people will encounter these portraits, enjoy  them and be inspired to think about their own stories, and their personal relationship to landscape.”
Tickets for the Great Yorkshire Show are on  sale now and are available to purchase in advance only, with prices frozen at the same rate as last year.
Free shuttle buses are operated by The Harrogate Bus Company every day of the Great Yorkshire Show. The buses run every 10 minutes from Harrogate Bus Station (next to the Railway Station) to the Showground’s White Gate, starting at 7.20am. The last bus departs the Showground at 7pm.

stories.title: The menopause was designed to isolate and separate. To strip away our looks, our confidence, our zest for life, and drive us indoors. My response? “Sod that.” And two events I attended last week showed me lots of other women are saying the same.
stories.body:
Mythos
This annual film conference brings together voices from the creative industries to help unlock opportunities and drive investment across the country, rather than just in the south. I came away BUZZING, especially after watching my clients - screenwriter Jan Birley and director Kathryn Carmichael - take part in a session alongside industry icon Helen Lederer about comedy, creativity and reinvention in later life. We were blown away by the amount of women who approached us afterwards to share their experiences of menopause and the fact that it either encouraged them or stopped them from trying new things  The whole session reinforced the ethos that if we stick together, we can deliver on the need for more films, narratives and art created by women, for women. As we YBW members know, the days of competition are over - it’s collaboration that wins the day!
MANIfest
For some of us, the menopause is the time when rather than hiding away, we decide it’s now or never and follow that gentle voice that tells us what we should be pursuing. If you call it manifestation or visualisation and throw a few crystals in, it can be met with cynicism. But MANIfest is a safe space for dreamers – and last weekend there were several hundred of them under one roof, with amazing speakers such as Tamu Thomas and Francesca Amber. The event was expertly organised by Mart Tweedy, who I used to work with in the NHS and is now a best-selling author who regularly tops the podcast charts. And all because he followed his dream.
Both events are a prime example of what’s possible if you get clear on your vision, believe it’s possible and take aligned action. I leapt into self-employment – totally scared witless - in my forties. I’m now 50 and working on projects I could never have dreamed of had I not taken that first step.
So, M does NOT just stand for menopause. It stands for Mythos. It stands for MANIfest. But most of all it stands for movement. Move fearfully or intentionally. If you move in the right direction. And don’t let anything stop you, especially a certain word beginning with M.

Claire Kenny is a ghostwriter, PR, and 50 plus blogger. Her insta is @writeforyou444

M does NOT just stand for menopause!

stories.title: In the heart of West Tanfield, at the foothills of the Yorkshire Dales, a new kind of wellbeing centre is preparing to open its doors — one rooted not just in science, but in lived experience, compassion and community.
stories.body:
Flow Yorkshire, has been created by Neil and Allanah Bourne-Arton, after decades navigating ADHD, Autism and related health challenges within their own family. What began as a deeply personal search for answers and meaningful support, has grown into a community-focused initiative, designed to help other individuals and families feel less alone, as clarity and healing become possible.
Flow is also a response to the significant barriers and waiting times many individuals and families face when seeking timely support through statutory services. To this end, the Flow Foundation has been established to ensure that financial barriers do not prevent families from receiving timely support. Five percent of every paid therapy programme within Flow, as well as ongoing dedicated fundraising, will go directly towards subsidising treatment for those who need it most, delivering support with dignity and care.
“We remember what it felt like to be overwhelmed, exhausted and unsure where to turn,” say the founders. “When we found approaches that truly helped our son and restored balance to our family, we knew we had to create a space where others could access that same support.”

Offering non-invasive, integrative therapies focused on calming the nervous system, reducing inflammation, supporting the gut-brain connection and restoring regulation, Flow’s services will support individuals and families experiencing anxiety, depression, neurodivergence, low mood, sleep challenges and sensory overwhelm, and all that arises as a result.

Therapies offered include:

Neurosonic - low-frequency sound and vibration therapy
Neuromodulation - supporting the Central and Autonomic Nervous System
Pulsed Electromagnetic Field (PEMF) therapy – grounding, reducing inflammation and improving circulation
Red Light Therapy - reducing inflammation and improving circulation, stimulating energy production from the cells
Health screening and genetic testing
Audio Visual Entrainment (AVE) - Sound and light therapy
Counselling and Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT) - for children and adults

Nutritional guidance
Medicinal Herbs
Holistic Life Coaching

While the centre will primarily serve the local community, remote consultations will also be available. Those choosing to travel from further afield, to use the modalities on offer within Flow, will benefit from priority rates at beautiful riverside accommodation, within walking distance of the centre.

A new wellbeing sanctuary, with a mission to widen access, opens in North Yorkshire

stories.title: On Wednesday 10 June, Kirklees College hosted its annual College Outstanding Learner Success Awards (COLS) at Accu Stadium in Huddersfield.
stories.body:
At the event, kindly sponsored by Calderdale and Huddersfield NHS Trust, 29 awards were presented across all of our subject areas, and each was awarded to a student who has gone above and beyond in their academic and extra-curricular efforts at college this year. The nominations were varied, and a lot of our students have excelled both in and out of the classroom.
The awards are funded through the Thomas Armytage Technical School Endowment of 1879, and every one of our winners received a cash prize in recognition of their efforts.
Three of our awards were based on our Kirklees College values of Kindness, Unity and Excellence. Our other two were a Students’ Choice, selected by our Students’ Union, and Principal’s Choice chosen by the college Principal Palvinder Singh.
Kirklees College Principal Palvinder Singh said: “Kirklees College’s mission statement is Creating Opportunities and Changing Lives. This fabulous event provides us with the opportunity to recognise our outstanding students who have grasped the opportunities that Kirklees College provides and changed their lives for the better.”
Have you been inspired?

To find out more about Kirklees College and to start your journey with us today, explore our range of courses and find out more.

Students and staff come together  to celebrate at COLS 2026

Our first event is a Novel Lunch on 6th May at the Ambers Restaurant within Cedar Court Hotel Harrogate. The event will commence at 12 noon and conclude at 3pm. It will be hosted by bestselling novelist, speaker and humourist Milly Johnson, who has authored over 20 novels. Milly will introduce our guest for the afternoon, another bestselling author Jane Costello. The lunch costs £55 for two courses and both authors will be signing books, either purchased on the day or brought by guests.
Following lunch at 3pm, we will be hosting our usual free member event. This will be attended by many members and their guests. A welcome drink will be served; complimentary canapés and a panel of speakers will be present. We will also be joined by This is Yorkshire Beauty and their members who will be discussing beauty topics. The event will conclude at 5pm.
Why not sign up for both and enjoy an afternoon of networking in beautiful surroundings whilst building your contacts?
Tennis fans? We have an upcoming event on 7th May at Ilkley Tennis Club, more details will be on our website shortly.
Yorkshire Businesswoman partners Pemberleys Restaurant will host an event with Charlotte Tilbury on Tuesday 12th May from 18:00 to 20:00. Tickets are £60 and include a welcome drink a two-course dinner with £30 of the ticket cost redeemed against products purchased on the evening. The event will be hosted by Yorkshire Businesswoman.
On 5th June, we will host our annual garden party at Goldsborough Hall in association with Lexus and Toyota. This complimentary event is open to members with guests paying a nominal fee of £10.
The occasion will feature a cream tea, entertainment and the opportunity for guests to test drive the latest Lexus car models. Please bring a valid driving licence if you wish to do so.

stories.title: Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons at Harewood House
stories.body:
Staging Icons offers a captivating journey through Beaton’s extraordinary career as a photographer to the stars. Featuring era-defining portraits such as those of Audrey Hepburn, Marilyn Monroe, Fred Astaire, Queen Elizabeth II and others, the show explores how Beaton’s spectacular approach to staging and design fashioned the identities of his all-star clientele, whilst also revealing Harewood’s own connections to the photographer’s world. From the opulence of the stage to the solemn grandeur of the monarchy, Staging Icons invites visitors to consider Beaton’s approach to image-making through the art of performance and how it continues to shape identity and the public imagination today.
Beaton’s first love was the stage, and it left a profound mark on his groundbreaking photographic style. Using elaborate backdrops, theatrical props, costume, lighting and dramatic composition, he blurred the line between reality and artifice, transforming each of his portraits into a work of visual theatre. His passion for creating opulent sets extended to stage and costume design, bringing his unique aesthetic to numerous operas and ballets, as well as stage and film productions in the post-war period.
Beaton’s bold creative vision was also instrumental in his work for the British Royal Family. Appointed royal photographer in 1939, his revolutionary portraits propelled the monarchy into the modern age, creating images that were both strikingly glamorous - demonstrated in Beaton’s portraits of Princess Margaret and Queen Elizabeth, The Queen Mother as well as remarkably intimate. Just like his work in show-business, Beaton’s royal portraiture combined elegance and control with theatre, spectacle and fantasy.
wStaging Icons also explores Harewood’s connection to Beaton and the world of stage and screen, featuring original portraits, artworks and archival material from the Harewood collection. Throughout his career, Beaton photographed numerous members of the Lascelles family and visited Harewood on several occasions during the 60s and early 70s.
Several portraits on display feature George and Patricia, 7th Earl and Countess of Harewood, who shared Beaton’s passion for the performing arts. George Harewood was a prominent figure in the world of music, and was a personal friend of many of Beaton’s sitters, such as the opera singer Maria Callas and composer Benjamin Britten.
Harewood’s historic visitor book, featuring Beaton’s signature, as well as a Christmas card with a hand-drawn costume design, appears in the show, documenting the photographer’s personal relationship with the family.
Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons is organised by the National Portrait Gallery in partnership with Harewood House Trust, marking their first collaborative project. Featuring several never-before displayed portraits, Staging Icons brings the National Portrait Gallery’s internationally significant Beaton collection to audiences in the North of England. Displayed throughout Harewood’s grand State Floor interiors, this major loan is presented against the backdrop Harewood’s own renowned
collection of British portraiture, including recent acquisitions such as Ashley Karrell’s powerful photographic portrait of British actor, David Harewood. This work was commissioned as part of Harewood’s Missing Portrait series that seeks to redress the lack of diversity within its historic portrait collection by commissioning new portraits of men and women of African-Caribbean heritage who have contemporary connections to Harewood.
Zoë Hughes, head of public programme at Harewood House Trust, commented: “Beaton’s visits to Harewood in the 60s and 70s is just one example of how the site has long been a source of inspiration for artists. Today, Harewood continuously reimagines what makes a historic country house, and by working with the National Portrait Gallery for the first time to bring together Beaton’s work and the House’s rich history, we offer our visitors the chance to step into his glamorous world and see icons, stars, and royals amongst the beautiful backdrop of the House.”
Miranda Stacey, head of national partnerships, National Portrait Gallery said “Beaton’s impact on portraiture, and how we visualise British culture, cannot be underestimated. Whether capturing a monarch or a muse, Beaton shaped the public imagination through his use of fantasy, beauty, and the art of performance.  Beaton and  the National Portrait Gallery share a long and distinguished history and his first major retrospective, Beaton Portraits, was presented here in 1968. It marked the first time the Gallery had ever exhibited photographs, and the first-time portraits of living sitters were shown, laying the foundations for our enduring
commitment to photography. We are thrilled to collaborate with Harewood House Trust to bring over 50 Beaton photographs from our collection to the North, giving audiences the chance to experience the creativity, elegance, and theatricality that defined Beaton’s work.”
Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons opens at Harewood House from 13 June to the 4 October 2026. For more information visit:
https://harewood.org/events/cecil-beaton-staging-icons/

Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons (13 June – 4 October 2026) brings together more than 50 unforgettable photographs from the National Portrait Gallery’s collection, some on display to the public for the first time.

stories.title: This summer, Yorkshire’s most vibrant country house, Harewood House Trust, hosts a major exhibition dedicated to the legendary photographer Cecil Beaton (1904 – 1980). Renowned for his innovative theatrical style, Beaton captured some of the most iconic figures of the 20th Century, from Hollywood legends and stars of the West End to the British Royal Family. Organised by the National Portrait Gallery in partnership with Harewood House Trust,
stories.body:
Staging Icons offers a captivating journey through Beaton’s extraordinary career as a photographer to the stars. Featuring era-defining portraits such as those of Audrey Hepburn, Marilyn Monroe, Fred Astaire, Queen Elizabeth II and others, the show explores how Beaton’s spectacular approach to staging and design fashioned the identities of his all-star clientele, whilst also revealing Harewood’s own connections to the photographer’s world. From the opulence of the stage to the solemn grandeur of the monarchy, Staging Icons invites visitors to consider Beaton’s approach to image-making through the art of performance and how it continues to shape identity and the public imagination today.
Beaton’s first love was the stage, and it left a profound mark on his groundbreaking photographic style. Using elaborate backdrops, theatrical props, costume, lighting and dramatic composition, he blurred the line between reality and artifice, transforming each of his portraits into a work of visual theatre. His passion for creating opulent sets extended to stage and costume design, bringing his unique aesthetic to numerous operas and ballets, as well as stage and film productions in the post-war period.
Beaton’s bold creative vision was also instrumental in his work for the British Royal Family. Appointed royal photographer in 1939, his revolutionary portraits propelled the monarchy into the modern age, creating images that were both strikingly glamorous - demonstrated in Beaton’s portraits of Princess Margaret and Queen Elizabeth, The Queen Mother as well as remarkably intimate. Just like his work in show-business, Beaton’s royal portraiture combined elegance and control with theatre, spectacle and fantasy.
wStaging Icons also explores Harewood’s connection to Beaton and the world of stage and screen, featuring original portraits, artworks and archival material from the Harewood collection. Throughout his career, Beaton photographed numerous members of the Lascelles family and visited Harewood on several occasions during the 60s and early 70s.
Several portraits on display feature George and Patricia, 7th Earl and Countess of Harewood, who shared Beaton’s passion for the performing arts. George Harewood was a prominent figure in the world of music, and was a personal friend of many of Beaton’s sitters, such as the opera singer Maria Callas and composer Benjamin Britten.
Harewood’s historic visitor book, featuring Beaton’s signature, as well as a Christmas card with a hand-drawn costume design, appears in the show, documenting the photographer’s personal relationship with the family.
Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons is organised by the National Portrait Gallery in partnership with Harewood House Trust, marking their first collaborative project. Featuring several never-before displayed portraits, Staging Icons brings the National Portrait Gallery’s internationally significant Beaton collection to audiences in the North of England. Displayed throughout Harewood’s grand State Floor interiors, this major loan is presented against the backdrop Harewood’s own renowned
collection of British portraiture, including recent acquisitions such as Ashley Karrell’s powerful photographic portrait of British actor, David Harewood. This work was commissioned as part of Harewood’s Missing Portrait series that seeks to redress the lack of diversity within its historic portrait collection by commissioning new portraits of men and women of African-Caribbean heritage who have contemporary connections to Harewood.
Zoë Hughes, head of public programme at Harewood House Trust, commented: “Beaton’s visits to Harewood in the 60s and 70s is just one example of how the site has long been a source of inspiration for artists. Today, Harewood continuously reimagines what makes a historic country house, and by working with the National Portrait Gallery for the first time to bring together Beaton’s work and the House’s rich history, we offer our visitors the chance to step into his glamorous world and see icons, stars, and royals amongst the beautiful backdrop of the House.”
Miranda Stacey, head of national partnerships, National Portrait Gallery said “Beaton’s impact on portraiture, and how we visualise British culture, cannot be underestimated. Whether capturing a monarch or a muse, Beaton shaped the public imagination through his use of fantasy, beauty, and the art of performance.  Beaton and  the National Portrait Gallery share a long and distinguished history and his first major retrospective, Beaton Portraits, was presented here in 1968. It marked the first time the Gallery had ever exhibited photographs, and the first-time portraits of living sitters were shown, laying the foundations for our enduring
commitment to photography. We are thrilled to collaborate with Harewood House Trust to bring over 50 Beaton photographs from our collection to the North, giving audiences the chance to experience the creativity, elegance, and theatricality that defined Beaton’s work.”
Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons opens at Harewood House from 13 June to the 4 October 2026. For more information visit:
https://harewood.org/events/cecil-beaton-staging-icons/

Cecil Beaton: Staging Icons (13 June – 4 October 2026) brings together more than 50 unforgettable photographs from the National Portrait Gallery’s collection, some on display to the public for the first time.

stories.title: Dean Clough, the 22-acre mixed-use destination in Halifax, has been selected for the second time as the main production base for hit TV show High Hoops.
stories.body:
The award-winning comedy, which stars Peep Show’s Robert Webb and Isy Suttie, previously utilised the Dean Clough mill complex as a base for operations for the second series.
Independent production company, Can Can Productions has secured a temporary lease for 5,000 sq ft of space for storage, costumes and set production with filming due to commence next month.
Thea Burrows, head of production at Can Can Productions, said, “We are thrilled to start filming a third series for High Hoops which has been hugely successful. Dean Clough provides an ideal base for production since our set film locations, such as Calderdale College, are so close by.
“The team at Dean Clough are extremely accommodating and flexible to our ever-changing production requirements, with 24-hour accessibility and a secure compound for our vehicles.”
Halifax is rapidly emerging as a significant centre for international film and television production, drawing major companies such as the BBC, Netflix, and Disney Plus to produce and film various projects. Dean Clough, in particular, has served as a filming location for well-known productions including Happy Valley, Ackley Bridge, and Secret Invasion, and has also featured as a backdrop for numerous presenting programmes.
Dean Clough is well known for its cultural contributions, featuring a distinctive underground theatre and one of the largest private art galleries in the UK. The Viaduct Theatre hosts Northern Broadsides, a celebrated resident company whose diverse touring schedule includes Shakespearean works, adaptations of classic plays, and fresh scripts delivered in a northern style, attracting a devoted audience.
It is also a prominent hub for creative industries, serving as home to Calderdale College’s digital creative skills centre, which offers state-of-the-art facilities for studies in film and TV production, design and editing, esports, and games design. Additionally, Dean Clough houses the UK’s largest collection of historic cinema projection equipment in its basement.
Jeremy Hall, chairman and managing director of Dean Clough, added, “We are delighted to have Can Can Productions and the High Hoops cast join us again at Dean Clough. We love working with them! It adds additional vibrancy and excitement to the community here requiring a quick-fire plan to deliver their filming location as it progresses!
“Dean Clough offers a  lively and diverse range of amenities that, together with its cultural attractions, creates a truly unique environment. You’ll find excellent cafes, bars, restaurants, and fitness, wellbeing, beauty, therapy, and retail services alongside six public art galleries, a subterranean theatre, art studios, and event venues—all within welcoming public spaces and plenty of onsite parking.”
Dean Clough, recipient of the prestigious ‘Development of the Year’ award at the 2024 Placemaking Awards, stands out
as one of Yorkshire’s largest and most influential centres dedicated to the business of arts and culture. The site actively supports commercial enterprise, educational practices, public engagement, and innovation across a wide array of disciplines.
Within the historic mills, the Arts Charity at Dean Clough (ACDC) plays a pivotal role in fostering inclusive arts, business, and education programmes. ACDC’s commitment to accessibility and community engagement was recognised with its own Placemaking award, underlining its contribution to the local and regional arts landscape.

Dean Clough to host production for third series of hit TV show

stories.title: People can now group collect, fee-free, for the Bradford City Centre Gift Card. Business leaders say they hope the move will divert teacher gift spending into the local economy.
stories.body:
A new partnership between Bradford BID, Miconex, and GiftRound will simplify group collecting for the Bradford City Centre Gift Card in time for the end of the school year.
From now, people will be able to set up a group collection for the Bradford City Centre Gift Card www.bradfordgiftcard.com and redeem as a digital gift card, fee-free.
Once the GiftRound collection closes, the digital Bradford City Centre Gift Card can be sent directly to the organiser with a printable gift card, or directly to the recipient with a link to messages from the contributors. Collections can also be split across multiple gifts/recipients.
Delivered by Bradford BID, the Bradford Gift Card is a local gift card that can be spent with over 120 local businesses including big brands like Primark, J D Sports and Nandos, and local independent businesses like Pizza Pieces, The Alhambra and Blossoms.
The ability to group collect for a Bradford Gift Card arrives just in time for the end of term teacher gifting season. With an estimated 4.5m children in state funded primary schools in the UK and parents spending an average of £9.06 on teacher gifts, over £4m is spent on teacher gifts at the end of term, and again at Christmas, if just 10% of families buy a gift for a teacher.
The partnership between Miconex and GiftRound will enable group collecting for over 90+ Town & City Gift Card programmes in the UK. Miconex announced over £24m in local gift card sales across its network in 2025, a 60% increase on 2024 as shoppers make the move to local gift cards to show support for local businesses.
Group collecting platform GiftRound reports that the average contribution to a teacher gift on their platform is £10, with an average overall teacher collection value of £145. The tech firm was founded in 2018, giving people an easy, fee-free way to collect money for various occasions including leaving gifts, new babies, weddings and teacher gifts.
Over 175,000 collections have been created to date through GiftRound with over £9 million in gift cards/vouchers ordered through the platform.
Nikki Chadburn, business communications manager at Bradford BID said: “The Bradford City Centre Gift Card is popular for gifts, rewards and incentives, giving the recipient money to spend with over      FF
120 amazing local businesses right here in Bradford city centre.The ability to group collect, gather messages in one place, and send our local gift card easily is a positive development and will be useful for end of term gifts but also wedding gifts, leaving gifts, new baby gifts and more. With over £4m being spent on teacher gifts at the end of term, the more spend we can lock into our economy through the Bradford City Centre Gift Card, the more businesses we can keep open and the more vibrant our city will be.”
Craig Forsythe, CEO & founder at GiftRound, said: “Going live with Town & City Gift Cards, including the Bradford City Centre Gift Card, across 90+ UK towns and cities is exciting. We’re making it easy to group collect for all kinds of occasions and redeem as a local gift card where the beneficiaries are the local businesses who power our economy. Gifts for teachers is one of our most popular collections and it makes huge sense. Instead of teachers receiving multiple mugs or gift sets, parents can each put a small amount into a collection, and the teacher receives the collection as a gift card to buy something they’d really like.”
David O’Neil, chief customer and growth officer at Miconex, said: “The Bradford Gift Card is the ultimate ‘shop local’ gift card, blending unbeatable local choice and unrivalled support for local. It’s fantastic to see this new technology go live in time for the end of term – helping people easily collect money as a group for a teacher’s gift – and gift a teacher the best of local in one gift card.”

New partnership rounds up ‘shop local’ gifts for the end of term

stories.title: Major exhibition announced for 2027 at Bolton Castle
stories.body:
Mary Queen of Scots: The Exhibition opens in March next year and is the first time the exhibition will be seen at a location where Mary Stuart was held.  Exploring the life, legacy and enduring fascination of Mary Queen of Scots, this captivating exhibition offers visitors a journey through the dramatic life of one of history’s most iconic and controversial monarchs, tracing her story from her birth in 1542 to her execution in 1587.  The exhibition combines original historical artefacts, immersive displays and striking visual design to bring her story vividly to life.
The exhibition’s arrival at Bolton Castle holds historical significance. Bolton Castle has long shown rooms dressed for Mary Queen of Scots’ stay of six months – often thought to be her longest period of captivity. Her continual imprisonment and eventual beheading is seen as one of the most controversial periods of British history and Bolton Castle has always honoured her stay in Wensleydale.
With displays housed within museum-grade display cases and enhanced by atmospheric graphics and period styling, the exhibition presents a compelling narrative of triumph, intrigue and tragedy. Visitors will encounter a rich collection of objects and interpretive materials that illuminate Mary’s life, reign and legacy, one that has captivated audiences for over four centuries.
Jacqui Naylor, general manager adds: “This is a tremendous opportunity for the castle to host such an acclaimed exhibition and to show visitors many original artefacts which were touched by Mary, such as her rosary, a lock of hair and tapestries which are thought to have been sewn by her very hands. In addition, costumes and immersive displays bring the story alive, all now to be seen for the first time within the walls of rooms she may have known well.  The exhibition also brings resonance to her sad story and a glimpse behind the royal facade at the woman herself. We are immensely proud to have this stunning exhibition from March to September and look forward to 2027.” 
Exhibition curator, John White said: ‘We are absolutely delighted to be staging Mary Queen of Scots: The Exhibition at Bolton Castle. The first castle to host our exhibition and a place that she knew well.’ 
Mary Queen of Scots: The Exhibition has been widely  praised for its ability to transport audiences into the past, combining authentic artefacts, detailed storytelling and evocative staging to create an engaging  and educational experience.  From royal intrigue and political rivalry to personal tragedy and enduring myth,  the exhibition explores the many dimensions of Mary’s life, offering fresh insights for both history enthusiasts and general visitors alike. 
Mary Queen of Scots: The Exhibition opens 6 March and runs to 5 September 2027.

Tickets are available now for 2027 from: Mary Queen of Scots: The Exhibition | Bolton Castle

Mary Queen of Scots Portrait with Gown at Bolton Castle in 2027
